title: PR #172: NonErgodicGenerativeProcess & InflatedVocabularyProcess
description: Walkthrough of two new generative process types: a block-diagonal nonergodic mixture model and a vocabulary inflation wrapper, plus builder functions and comprehensive tests.

steps.title: Overview: Two new generative process abstractions
steps.body:
This PR introduces two new `GenerativeProcess` subclasses:

1. **NonErgodicGenerativeProcess** — A block-diagonal mixture model that composes multiple `GenerativeProcess` components with weighted probabilities. No transitions occur between components; beliefs are updated via Bayesian filtering.

2. **InflatedVocabularyProcess** — A wrapper that multiplies vocabulary size by a factor K with uniform noise, increasing optimal per-token loss by exactly `log(K)` nats.

The PR also adds 9 builder functions for constructing these processes from YAML specs, supporting disjoint and partially-overlapping vocabulary configurations with three mapping strategies (prefix, sliding, random).

Key design choices:
- Does NOT materialize a full block-diagonal matrix — stores component processes directly for efficiency
- Uses `IndependentFactoredGenerativeProcess` for independent structures to achieve O(sum V_i) sampling complexity
- Handles heterogeneous state types (HMM vs Factored) via flatten/pad/unflatten for `jax.lax.switch` compatibility

steps.title: NonErgodicState: the composite state representation
steps.body:
The state is a `NamedTuple` with two fields:
- `component_beliefs`: a probability distribution over components, shape `[num_components]`. During generation this becomes one-hot after the first emission; during inference it's updated via Bayes rule.
- `component_states`: a tuple of per-component states, where each element can be either a flat `jax.Array` (HMM) or a tuple of arrays (FactoredState).

This heterogeneous state design is central to the PR — it allows mixing different process types (HMM, GHMM, Factored) in a single mixture.

steps.title: State flattening utilities for JAX compatibility
steps.body:
Since `jax.lax.switch` requires all branches to return identically-shaped arrays, these three helper functions flatten heterogeneous component states into uniform 1D arrays:

- `_get_flat_size`: counts total elements
- `_flatten_state`: concatenates to 1D
- `_unflatten_state`: reconstructs original structure using a template

Note the use of `jax.lax.dynamic_slice` instead of Python slicing in `_unflatten_state` (line 71) — this avoids `ConcretizationTypeError` inside `jax.lax.switch` since template shapes are known at trace time.

steps.title: Constructor: vocab maps and inverse maps
steps.body:
The constructor normalizes component weights, builds forward vocab maps (local-to-global), and computes inverse maps (global-to-local) for efficient observation routing.

Key details:
- Weights are normalized to sum to 1 (line 156)
- If no vocab maps provided, each component gets identity mapping `[0..V-1]` (line 160)
- The unified vocab size is `max(all global tokens) + 1` (line 163)
- Inverse maps use `-1` sentinel for unmapped tokens (line 167), which `transition_states` checks to determine if an observation belongs to a component

comments.author: Eric Alt
Eric Alt
comments.body:
```python
  Raises:
      ValueError: If components is empty, weights don't match component count,
          or a component vocab_map contains duplicate global token indices

...

  for i, vm in enumerate(vocab_maps):
      if len(set(vm)) != len(vm):
          raise ValueError(f"vocab_maps[{i}] must not contain duplicate global token indices")
```

Corresponding unit tests should also be added

steps.title: Observation distribution: weighted mixture over components
steps.body:
Computes `P(obs | state) = sum_i P(component_i | state) * P(obs | component_i, state_i)`.

For each component: gets the local distribution, scatters it into global vocab space via `vocab_maps[i]`, and weights by `component_beliefs[i]`. Tokens not in a component's vocab naturally get probability 0.

The log-space variant (line 208-228) uses `logsumexp` across stacked per-component log distributions for numerical stability, with `-inf` for unmapped tokens.

comments.author: Eric Alt
comments.body: `len(self.components)` should always equal `len(self.vocab_maps)` so `strict` should be `True` instead of `False`

steps.title: Bayesian filtering in transition_states
steps.body:
This is the core inference logic. For each observation:

1. Map global token to each component's local space via inverse vocab maps (line 268)
2. Compute likelihood `P(obs | component_i)` — 0 if token not in component's vocab (lines 271-275)
3. Conditionally update each component's internal state only when likelihood > 0 (lines 278-284)
4. Apply Bayes rule: `new_beliefs = beliefs * likelihoods / normalizer` (lines 288-294)
5. Fall back to prior beliefs if all likelihoods are 0 (line 291-293)

The `jax.lax.cond` on line 278 avoids unnecessary state transitions for components that couldn't have generated the observation.

steps.title: Generation: sample one component, generate entire sequence
steps.body:
Unlike inference (which tracks beliefs across all components), generation samples a single component at the start and generates entirely from it.

The implementation is notable for its complexity:
- Cannot delegate to `component.generate()` because that method is also vmapped (line 363)
- Uses flatten/pad to a common max size so `jax.lax.switch` can handle heterogeneous state types (lines 389-398)
- `scan_step` (line 411) runs generation via `lax.switch` selecting the active component
- Only the active component's state is updated per step (lines 424-431)
- When `return_all_states=True`, a second inference scan reconstructs belief trajectories (lines 447-453)

This is the most intricate part of the PR — the flatten/pad/unflatten dance is the price paid for supporting mixed component types in a single JIT-compiled scan.

comments.author: Eric Alt
comments.body: TODO: instead of having generative processes's `generate` function vmapped by default we should just have it function for a single sequence and define a separete `generate_batch` function that just wraps the generate function in a `vmap` (or just require the caller of generate to do that themselves) - outside the scope of this PR though

steps.title: InflatedVocabularyProcess: controlled difficulty via noise
steps.body:
A clean decorator pattern that wraps any `GenerativeProcess[State]` to inflate its vocabulary.

Token encoding: `inflated_token = noise_prefix * V_base + base_token`
- `emit_observation` samples a base token then adds a uniform random noise prefix (lines 68-71)
- `transition_states` extracts the base token via modulo and discards noise (line 76)
- Probability distributions are tiled K times and divided by K (line 83)
- `probability` applies a `(1/K)^T` penalty for a sequence of length T (line 96)

This elegantly increases optimal per-token loss by exactly `log(K)` nats while preserving all state dynamics.

steps.title: Generator updates: slicing NonErgodicState belief trajectories
steps.body:
The existing `generate_data_batch_with_full_history` function needed to handle `NonErgodicState` when slicing belief trajectories along the sequence dimension.

A new `_slice_belief_states` helper (line 96) handles three state representations:
- Plain arrays: slice directly
- Tuples of arrays: slice each element
- `NonErgodicState`: slice both `component_beliefs` and each entry in `component_states`, handling nested tuples for factored components (line 111)

This replaces the previous inline isinstance/tuple handling with a cleaner dispatch.

steps.title: IndependentFactoredGenerativeProcess: noise_epsilon passthrough
steps.body:
A small but important change: `noise_epsilon` is added as a constructor parameter (line 52) and forwarded to the parent `FactoredGenerativeProcess.__init__` (line 83).

This allows nonergodic processes to compose factored components that use noisy channels — previously the `IndependentFactoredGenerativeProcess` would silently ignore this parameter.

steps.title: Builder: component factory and nonergodic process construction
steps.body:
The private `_build_components_from_spec` helper (line 653) is the foundation for all nonergodic builders. It dispatches on `component_type` to build HMM, GHMM, or Factored processes.

`build_nonergodic_process_from_spec` (line 700) is the main entry point, documented with a full YAML example showing how to compose HMM, GHMM, and factored components with explicit vocab maps.

Note how vocab maps can be specified either per-component in the spec or globally as an override (lines 754-761).

steps.title: Builder: vocabulary mapping strategies
steps.body:
Three vocab map strategies for partially overlapping alphabets:

1. **Prefix** (line 805): C0 gets `[0..V-1]`, subsequent components share a prefix of `n_shared` tokens plus unique tokens above V
2. **Sliding** (line 820): Each component's vocab slides by `max(1, n_unique)` tokens — simple offset strategy
3. **Random** (line 829): Each component independently samples V tokens from the global pool using a seeded RNG

`build_nonergodic_partial_overlap` (line 842) orchestrates these, computing `n_shared = int(V * overlap_frac)` and `n_unique = V - n_shared` from the `overlap_frac` parameter. All components must have equal vocab size for this to work (validated on line 875-876).

`build_nonergodic_disjoint_vocab` (line 771) is the simpler case: sequential non-overlapping ranges `[0..V0-1], [V0..V0+V1-1], ...`

comments.author: Eric Alt
Eric Alt
comments.body: `assert seed is not None` is redundant with earlier check

comments.author: Eric Alt
comments.body:
```python
  def _build_random_vocab_maps(n_components: int, v: int, n_unique: int, seed: int) -> list[list[int]]:
      """Build vocab maps by having each component randomly sample V tokens from the global pool.

      The global vocab size is the same as in prefix mode:
      V + (n_components - 1) * n_unique.
      """
      global_vocab_size = v + (n_components - 1) * n_unique
      rng = random.Random(seed)

      return [
          sorted(rng.sample(range(global_vocab_size), v))
          for _ in range(n_components)
      ]
```

steps.title: Builder: InflatedVocabularyProcess construction
steps.body:
Two builder functions for the inflation wrapper:

- `build_inflated_process` (line 900): Simple wrapper taking an existing `GenerativeProcess` and inflation factor
- `build_inflated_process_from_spec` (line 916): Builds the base process from a spec dict first, then wraps it — supports HMM, GHMM, and factored base types

Both are thin wrappers that delegate to the `InflatedVocabularyProcess` constructor.

steps.title: build_factored_process now returns IndependentFactoredGenerativeProcess
steps.body:
A structural change in the existing `build_factored_process` function: when `structure_type == "independent"`, it now returns an `IndependentFactoredGenerativeProcess` directly (line 200) with early return, rather than falling through to the generic `FactoredGenerativeProcess` constructor.

This ensures that nonergodic processes composing independent factored components get the specialized subclass with per-factor sampling and frozen factor support, plus the new `noise_epsilon` passthrough.

steps.title: Summary and recommendations
steps.body:
**Strengths:**
- Clean abstractions: both new classes implement the full `GenerativeProcess` protocol
- The flatten/pad/unflatten approach for heterogeneous states in `jax.lax.switch` is well-documented and correct
- Comprehensive builder functions with three vocab mapping strategies cover real research use cases
- Excellent test coverage (21+ tests for NonErgodic, tests for Inflated, builder tests)
- The `InflatedVocabularyProcess` is a particularly elegant design — stateless noise with provable loss increase

**Architecture notes:**
- The `generate` method in `NonErgodicGenerativeProcess` is the most complex piece — the re-implementation of the generate loop (rather than delegating to components) is necessary due to vmap constraints but adds maintenance burden
- The `_slice_belief_states` helper in `generator.py` adds a third branch for `NonErgodicState` — if more state types emerge, this could benefit from a protocol-based dispatch

**Recommendation: Approve** — Well-structured addition with solid test coverage and clear documentation of the tricky JAX compatibility patterns.
